summary: Julia Evans describes her evolving relationship with design documents, moving from grumpiness about writing them to finding them genuinely useful. She identifies common frustrations — nobody reads them, feedback can be unconstructive bikeshedding, and they can't predict everything. Her key shift was reframing design docs as personal thinking tools and conversation starters rather than formal artifacts requiring thorough review. She also values them as archaeological records that explain past decisions when revisited months later.
key_points: Design documents work better as personal preparation for productive conversations than as formal review artifacts people are expected to read thoroughly
Getting useful feedback requires targeted asks — directing specific questions to specific people rather than broadcasting the whole document
The inability to predict everything in advance doesn't invalidate design docs; they're about doing your best thinking upfront, not achieving perfection
Design documents serve as valuable archaeological artifacts that explain why decisions were made when revisited months or years later
Asking more people for feedback isn't always better — sometimes one or two reviewers is enough for a small project
money_quotes: I have written extensive notes so that I can make sure to ask you all the most important questions I have and we can have a SUPER PRODUCTIVE MEETING.
This idea that you can predict all the flaws in advance and design the perfect system is actually a total lie.
The reason you write them is because your requirements & ideas of how things should work will change over time, and it's useful to track that.
Often I'll make a design decision, forget about it, and then 8 months later be like 'ugh who made that decision and why???'. And then it was me.
Design documents are cool as archaeological artifacts so that people from the future can understand you and why you decided what you did.
Asking more people for feedback isn't always better. Sometimes asking just one or two people is ok if it's a small thing.
Hey just read this for 10 minutes and see if anything makes you EXTREMELY TERRIFIED AND WORRIED.
key_insight: Design documents are most valuable not as formal review artifacts but as personal thinking tools, conversation starters, and archaeological records of why decisions were made.
